09 / 2020 © 2020 KOSTAL Solar Electric GmbH 
1.	Introduction
1.1	MODBUS Protocol
The following describes the functionality of the Modbus App in the form of possible configuration, ope­
rating modes and the Modbus register specification. A detailed description of the Modbus protocol and 
its operation can be found in the Modbus specification (see www.modbus.org). Modbus TCP is part of 
the IEC 61158 standard.
The Modbus data interface can be used in the following operating modes:
	
n
Modbus RTU Slave
	
n
Modbus RTU Master
	
n
Modbus TCP Slave
	
n
Modbus TCP Master 
1.2	Modbus RTU via RS485 interface
In Modbus RTU Slave mode, the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter provides its Modbus registers via 
RS485. Both RS485 interfaces, RS485 A and RS485 B, can be individually configured for this purpose. 
Details on the connection to the RS-485 socket and the polarity reversal of the interface can be found in 
the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter installation manual.
Termination

RS485. Both RS485 interfaces, RS485 A and RS485 B, can be individually configured for this purpose. 
Details on the connection to the RS-485 socket and the polarity reversal of the interface can be found in 
the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter installation manual.
Termination
Depending on the cable length and the number of participants on the bus, it is recommended terminate 
the bus at both ends with a 120 ohm resistor. By the termination will reduce reflections in the connec­
tion. This may happen be absolutely necessary for the reliability of the application.
Settings
First, the slot of the bus and the associated interface must interface RS485 A or RS485 B interface 
must be clarified. The settings for the respective RS-485 are activated via the interface Interface activa­
ted. To start communication via the interface, the Settings can still be saved with Save.
Configuration of the interface

must be clarified. The settings for the respective RS-485 are activated via the interface Interface activa­
ted. To start communication via the interface, the Settings can still be saved with Save.
Configuration of the interface
The specific configuration depends on the requirements of the remote site. In doubt the configuration of 
the remote station should be consulted. 
	
n
Presetting can contain a selection of preset parameters.

1
09 / 2020 © 2020 KOSTAL Solar Electric GmbH 
Advanced:
	
n
Modus configures the interface to be either a Modbus master or slave function
	
n
Slave address (Modbus slave) sets the address of the Energy Manager in Modbus. This Value can 
be a value between 1 and 255.
	
n
Baud rate sets the baud rate of the connection.
	
n
Data bits sets the number of data bits.
	
n
Parity sets the parity of the connection.
	
n
Stop bits sets the number of stop bits.
	
n
Slave addresses (Modbus master) provides one or more addresses of Modbus slaves on. Each 
address can have a value between 1 and 255 and may only be used once be used.
1.3	Modbus TCP via Ethernet interface
TCP Master
In Modbus TCP Master mode, the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter writes in the registers of one or more 
configured slaves. The internal instantaneous value registers can be written and the internal energy 
value register (see register specification). The KOSTAL / RM PnP register and the SunSpec registers are

configured slaves. The internal instantaneous value registers can be written and the internal energy 
value register (see register specification). The KOSTAL / RM PnP register and the SunSpec registers are 
not transferred via Modbus masters. 
	
n
Slave address sets the address of a TCP slave. This can be specified in the form of an IP address or 
a URL.
	
n
Port sets the TCP port on which the slave expects Modbus communication. Up to 10 TCP slaves 
can be configured.
TCP - Slave
In Modbus TCP Slave mode, the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter provides its Modbus registers over TCP/
IP. Access to this interface requires network connection via Ethernet. The Modbus slave can be reached 
in accordance with the industry standard under port number 502.
	
n
Activate TCP slave activates and deactivates the Modbus slave functionality.

2
09 / 2020 © 2020 KOSTAL Solar Electric GmbH 
1.4	Extended Modbus configuration
Additional settings for Modbus masters can be made here. These settings are valid for all configured 
Modbus masters.
Send interval
A push interval can be configured here. The Energy Manager normally sends its measurement data via 
Modbus master as soon as it is available. If the push interval is activated, the data is instead sent at a 
regular interval, regardless of when they become available. Only the current measured values ​are shown 
here transmitted at the time of transmission, no averages over the interval are formed.
Activate fixed transmission interval switches the push interval on. There must then be a transmission 
interval either selected from the selection list (from 1 second to 1 day) or user-defined in seconds in 
which the Energy Manager sends its data should.
Register configuration
Here groups of registers can be selected that are written in master mode should be. This can reduce

interval either selected from the selection list (from 1 second to 1 day) or user-defined in seconds in 
which the Energy Manager sends its data should.
Register configuration
Here groups of registers can be selected that are written in master mode should be. This can reduce 
the system load if not all registers are required become. The following can be selected:
	
n
Total instantaneous values: The instantaneous values ​of the overall system (registers 0–27)
	
n
Instantaneous values ​phases: The instantaneous values ​broken down by phases (register 40-145)
	
n
Total energy values: The energy values ​of the overall system (registers 512–551)
	
n
Phases energy values: The energy values ​broken down by phases (register 592–791)
A detailed list of the tabs can be opened using the arrow on the right that are contained in the respec­
tive groups.

3
09 / 2020 © 2020 KOSTAL Solar Electric GmbH 
2.	MODBUS protocol description
2.1	Register Specification
The data registers can be divided into different areas. The data points of the KOSTAL Smart Energy 
Meter are coded according to the OBIS standard. In addition, in the register area 40000-40177, the 
data points are encoded according to SunSpec Alliance standards:
	
n
SunSpec Alliance Interoperability Specification – Common Models
	
n
SunSpec Alliance Interoperability Specification – Meter Models 
2.2	Reading out registers
Most data points of the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter are distributed to several 16-bit registers. This 
means that an RTU master / TCP client should request all registers of a data point in one and the same 
request.
Interpretation of data points with multiple registers: In the case of a multi-register data point, the regis­
ters with the lower address contain the „mustsignificant“ bits. The „least siginificant“ bits are contained

request.
Interpretation of data points with multiple registers: In the case of a multi-register data point, the regis­
ters with the lower address contain the „mustsignificant“ bits. The „least siginificant“ bits are contained 
in the registers with the higher address.
Example Principle: A fictitious data point „TotalOperatingHours“ (uint32) is located at offset 0x1000. The 
data point should contain 2293828 operating hours.
	
n
the address 0x1000 contains 0x23
	
n
the address 0x1001 contains 0x44
During the request, both registers are transmitted in the network byte order (Big Endian) as specified by 
the Modbus specification. A „Read Holding Register“ for both registers provides 0x00 0x23 0x00 0x44.
Example conversion: 
In order to read the referenced active power (+), the (integer) values of the „holding registers“ 0 and 1 
can be used:
Active power+ [W] = ({register 0} · 2^16 + {register 1}) · 0.1 [W]

Example conversion: 
In order to read the referenced active power (+), the (integer) values of the „holding registers“ 0 and 1 
can be used:
Active power+ [W] = ({register 0} · 2^16 + {register 1}) · 0.1 [W]
In order to read out the related active energy (+), that is to say the related active energy over all phases, 
the (integer) registers 512 to 515 can use:
Active energy+ [Wh] = ({register 512} · 2^48 + {register 513} · 2^32 + {register 514} · 2^16 + {register 
515}) · 0.1 [Wh]

4
09 / 2020 © 2020 KOSTAL Solar Electric GmbH 
2.3	OBIS key figures system
So-called OBIS codes are used to transfer data and differentiate between the various measurement 
data from a data source. OBIS stands for Object Identification System and is used for electronic data 
communication in the energy market.
OBIS key figures consist of six value groups (A-F) from the combination of which the speci ﬁ cation of a 
value is derived. They are shown in the form A-B: C.D.E * F.
The specific OBIS codes used in the Energy Manager are described in the document attachment 
depending on the data source. The OBIS key figure system in version 2.0 (status: 02.02.2009), which is 
based on DIN EN 62056-61: 2007-06 and can be found at edi-energy.de, serves as the basis. The indi­
vidual groups are explained below in the context of the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter. Note: The values 
of groups A and F are ﬁ xed, those of the remaining groups are variable.
Group A (medium)
A = 1 (electricity)

vidual groups are explained below in the context of the KOSTAL Smart Energy Meter. Note: The values 
of groups A and F are ﬁ xed, those of the remaining groups are variable.
Group A (medium)
A = 1 (electricity)
Group B (channel)
Used to differentiate the three possible data sources:
for „Smart Meter“ values: B = 0
for sensor values: B = sensor ID + 1
for group values: B = group ID + 100
Group C (measured variable)
Key value of the resulting measured variable according to the OBIS key figure system
Group D (measurement type)
Key value of the type of measurement used according to the OBIS key figure system
Group E (tariff level)
Key value of the tariff, mostly E = 0 (total)
Group F (pre-value counter)
F = 255

09 / 2020 © 2020 KOSTAL Solar Electric GmbH 
The KSEM/RM PnP register contains information on the identity of the device.
	
n
ManufacturerID is a static value that contains the ID of the manufacturer. An overriding SCADA sys­
tem can differentiate between different devices on the RS-485.
	
n
ProductID is also a static value that allows the identification of the specific product via this key.
	
n
ProductVersion is the version of the hardware of the product.
	
n
FirmwareVersion is the version of the software of the product.
	
n
VendorName and product name include the name of the manufacturer and the name of the product 
as strings.
All strings are filled to their full length by NUL bytes and spaces (0x32). The Modbus RTU Master / TCP 
Client should auto-cut them before using the strings.

1 Note to avoid off-by-one errors: The SunSpec specification (as found at www.sunspec.org) always refers to register numbers, whereas this docu­
ment always refers to register addresses. To access SunSpec register 40001, the register address 40000 must be used, i. Hexadecimal offset

0x9C40.
2 These fields may receive a customer fire request upon request
3 Example: The register M_AC_Freq contains the value 4950 and M_AC_Freq_SF contains the value -2. Then the frequency can be calculated as: 
4950 Hz * 10 ^ {- 2} = 49.50 Hz
4 Important Note: Although the scaling factors are given here as fixed values, they should not be considered fixed. The values can change dynami­
cally to match the readings. Please always ask the scaling factors together with the associated values and include code to dynamically calculate 
the values.

3.6	Overview group register
This register area contains group-specific information. There are a total of 48 blocks of group regis­
ters. Each register block has 40 registers and corresponds to a group that is con ﬁ gured in the Energy 
Manager. The group registers are only available if groups have been activated and con ﬁ gured on the 
device.
The o ﬀ set of each group register block is calculated as follows:
offset = 0xE800 + (Gruppen-ID) * 0x0028

3.7	Overview sensor register
This register area contains sensor-specific information. There are a total of 96 blocks of sensor regis­
ters. Each register block has a size of 40 registers and corresponds to a sensor that is connected to the 
Energy Manager. The sensor registers are only available if sensors are activated and con ﬁ gured on the 
device.
The o ﬀ set of each sensor register block is calculated as follows:
offset = 0xF000 + (Sensor-ID) * 0x0028

The register block is the same for all groups. Therefore, only the register block for the first sensor is 
described in the following table. The OBIS codes are used here for illustration purposes only, as the 
real OBIS code depends on the con ﬁ gured phase of the sensor. If the phase of a sensor has not been 
con ﬁ gured, its registers do not contain any values, since the phase is required to calculate the further 
values from the internal measured values with the help of the voltage and phase angle.
